folio.label: 168r
folio.l:
a spere , hold the oon the rigút syde , & if he bere a swerd , holde
the oon his lift syde ,

And after this , than shuln ©e kepe ©ow
wysely , from alle swicú manere peeple , as I haue seid bifore
and hem & here cou~seile eschue

¢ And after æis , æa~ne shuln ©e
kepe ©ow in swicú manere ,

æ for any päsumpciou~ of ©oure
strengthe , æ ©e ne dispise nat , ne attempte nat the mygút , of
©oure aduäsarie , so lite , æ ©e lete the kepyngö of ©oure ¹sone , for ©ourö
päsumpciou~ ,
for euäy wys man , dredetú his enemy ,

And Salamo~
seitú ; welful is he , that of alle hatú drede ,

For certes , he that
thurgú the hardynesse of his herte , & thurgú the hardynesse of
hi~ self , hatú to gret päsumpciou~ , hi~ shal yuel bitide ,,

Tha~ne
shuln ©e euäe mo , countrewayte emboyssementz , & alle espialle ,

For Senek seitú , æ the wyse man , æ dredetú harmes , eschewetú
harmes ,
ne he ne falletú in to ¹ils , æ ¹ils eschuetú

¢ And al be
it so , æ it seme , æ æè art in syker place , ©et shaltow alwey , do æi
diligence , in kepyngö of thy ¹sone ,

this is to seyn ; ne be nat
necligent to kepe thy ¹sone , nat oonly fro thyne grettest enemys ,
but fro thy leste enemy

¢ Senek seitú , a man æ is wel a vised , he
dredetú his leste enemy

¢ Ouyde seitú , æ the litel wesele , wol slee
the gret bole , & æe wylde hert

¢ And æe book seitú , A litel thorn~ , may
prikke a kyng ful sore , & an hound , wil holde the wilde Boor ,,

But
nathelees , I seye nat , æè shalt be so coward , æ æè doute there , wherö
as is no drede

¢ The book seitú , æ so~me folk han gret lust to de-
ceyue , but ©et they dreden he~ , to be deceyued

¢ ©et shaltow drede
to be empoysened , & kepe the fro the compaignye of skorneres ,

For the book seitú , w scorners make no compaignye , but flee herö
wordes , as venym

¢ Now as to the secund poynt , wherö as
©oure wyse cou~seilloås , cou~seilled ©ow , to warnestore ©oure hous , w
gret diligence ,

I wolde fayn knowe , how æ ©e vnderstode thilke
wordes , & what is ©oure sentence

¢ Melibeus answerde & seide ,
Certes I vnderstonde it , in æis wyse , æ I shal warnestore my~
hous , w toures , swicú as han Castelles , & othere manere edifices ,
& armure , & artelries ,

by whicú thynges , I may my ¹sone &
myn hous , so kepen & defenden , æ myne enemys , shuln ben in
drede , myn hous for to approche

¢ To this sentence , answerde
a noon Pèdence ; warnestoryngö q¶ she , of heigúe Toures , & of
grete edifices ,

w grete costages , & w gret tuaille , And whan
æ they ben acompliced , ©et be they nat wortú a stree , but if æei
ben defended , by trewe frendes , æ ben bolde & wyse ,

And vnder-
stonde wel , æ the gretteste & strongeste garnesou~ , æ a riche man
may haue , as wel to kepen his ¹sone , as hise goodes , is ,

æ he
be biloued w his subgetz , & w his neyghebores

¢ For thus seitú
Tullius ; That æäe is a manere garnesou~ , æ no man may venqésse
ne discomfite , & that is
a lord , to be biloued of his Citezeins , & of
